coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots amplify beneficial effects of EGCG against neural injuries by NLRP3 inflammasome after intracerebral hemorrhage
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Min
Pan, Yun
Tang, Shijin
Guo, Tingwang
Yang, Lin
Chen, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Neuroinflammation plays an indispensable role in neural damages after ICH, responsible for the induced high mortality and poor prognosis. NLRP3 inflammasome, which is known mediated by ROS, has been widely documented to aggravate brain injuries. Therefore, suppressing neural injuries by ROS/NLRP3 pathway may be beneficial in treating ICH. As the major catechin found in green tea, epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) shows excellent anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects. In this study, EGCG-carbon quantum dots (EGCG-CQDs) were successfully fabricated based on EGCG by hydrothermal synthesis method. EGCG-CQDs exhibited an excellent aqueous solubility, and emerged more abundant phenolic oxygens as well as oxygen-rich functional groups. Importantly, EGCG-CQDs showed superior free radical scavenging activity by DPPH and ABTS assays in vitro than EGCG. In vivo, a significant antioxidative activity was presented by EGCG-CQDs rather than EGCG. Furthermore, the upregulated NLRP3 and the induced inflammatory cascades (NF-κB, Caspase-1 and GSDMD) in ICH were attenuated by EGCG-CQDs. Inflammatory factor productions were also decreased by EGCG-CQDs, such as IL-1β, IL-18, IL-6 and TNF-α. Finally, the disturbed neural viability, disordered cytomorphology, and neurological deficits were significantly improved by EGCG-CQDs rather than EGCG. Therefore, CQDs might be an effective form to amplify the efficacy and bioavailability of EGCG, exerting considerable effects on treating ICH by suppressing ROS/NLRP3.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intracerebral haemorrhage
NLRP3 inflammasome
ROS
EGCG
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Primary cortical neuronal cultures and animal preparation
ROS, LDH, MDA, GSH and neuron viability
Synthesis and characterization of EGCG-derived CQDs
EGCG-CQDs suppressed oxidative stress related factors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11902060), the Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology (Grant Nos. cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0844 and cstc2020jcyjmsxmX0250), the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant No. KJQN201900814, KJQN202100837 and KJZD-K202400805) and the Opening Research Platform of Chongqing Technology and Business University (KFJJ2022016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of EGCG-CQDs (a), in vitro (b) and vivo (c) experimental design.

TEM images (a), diameter (b), zeta potential (c), FT-IR spectra (f), UV–vis absorption (g), and fluorescence spectra (h) of as-prepared EGCG-CQDs by hydrothermal synthesis method at 150 °C, 180 °C or 210 °C. Scale bar: 10 nm. d and e indicated the diameter and zeta potential of EGCG-CQDs-180. n = 6 experiments each group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM.

EGCG-CQDs showed scavenging activity in vitro and vivo. a and b, EGCG and EGCG-CQDs-150/180/210 (0–100 µM) were reacted with DPPH and ABTS. IC50 value was calculated by Graph Pad 9.0. c and d, the intracellular ROS in primary cortical neurons which were incubated with EGCG or EGCG-CQDs at 0.1, 1, 10 and 100 µM. c referred to the representative fluorescence images. Scale bar: 100 μm. d referred to the fluorescence intensity which was analyzed by Image-Pro Plus. e, ROS content in the perihematomal brain tissues. n = 6 experiments each group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005 vs Con or Sham group, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01, ### p < 0.005 vs Hb or ICH group, one-way ANOVA and post hoc Dunnett's test.

EGCG-CQDs reduced oxidative stress in vitro and vivo. a and d, the release of cytoplasmic LDH in vitro and vivo. b and e, the content of MDA. c and f, the content of GSH. g and h, the expression of Nrf-2, HO-1 and SOD2 in vitro. i and j, the expression of Nrf-2, HO-1 and SOD2 in vivo. n = 20 experiments each group in vitro and 6 experiments in vivo. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005 vs Con or Sham group, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01, ### p < 0.005 vs Hb or ICH group, one-way ANOVA and post hoc Dunnett's test.

EGCG-CQDs suppressed NLRP3 inflammasome after ICH. a and b, NLRP3-positive primary cortical cells in vitro. Neurons were immunostained with NLRP3 in green and DAPI in blue. Scale bar: 100 μm. c, NLRP3-positive neuronal cells in the perihematomal tissues in vivo. The neural tissues were immunostained with NLRP3 in green, NeuN in red and DAPI in blue. The dotted lines indicated the trace of the needle that was inserted stereotactically into the striatum. Scale bar: 50 μm. d-g, the expression of NLRP3 following different operations in vitro and vivo. n = 6 experiments each group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005 vs Con or Sham group, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01, ### p < 0.005 vs Hb or ICH group, one-way ANOVA and post hoc Dunnett's test.

EGCG-CQDs decreased the induced inflammatory effects. a and c, Tunel-positive primary cortical cells in vitro. Neurons were immunostained with Tunel in red and DAPI in blue. Scale bar: 100 μm. b, Caspase-1- or Tunel-positive neuronal cells in the perihematomal tissues in vivo. The neural tissues were immunostained with Caspase-1 in green, Tunel in red and DAPI in blue. The dotted lines indicated the trace of the needle that was inserted stereotactically into the striatum. Scale bar: 50 μm. d-g, the expression of NF-κB p-p65, NF-κB p65, p-IκBα, IκBα, Caspase-1 p20, pro-Caspase-1 and GSDMD. h, the level of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-18, IL-6 and TNF-α) after ICH. n = 6 experiments each group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005 vs Con or Sham group, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01, ### p < 0.005 vs Hb or ICH group, one-way ANOVA and post hoc Dunnett's test.

EGCG-CQDs improved neural cells viability and structure in vitro. a, cells viability was measured by CCK-8. n = 20 experiments each group. b, the mRNA expression of NeuN, MAP-2, NCAM-L1 and GAP-43. n = 6 experiments each group. c, cells were stained with MAP-2 (green) and DAPI (blue). d and e, the number of processes and mean dendrite length were performed by ImageJ. Scale bar: 100 μm. n = 120 cells per group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005 vs Con group, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01, ### p < 0.005 vs Hb group, one-way ANOVA and post hoc Dunnett's test.

EGCG-CQDs improved edema and neural functions after ICH. a, H&E staining was performed. The black arrows refered to the cell necrosis. Scale bar: 50 μm. b and c, brain water content in ipsilateral cortex (ipsi-cortex), ipsilateral basal ganglia (ipsi-basal ganglia), contralateral cortex (cont-cortex) and contralateral basal ganglia (cont-basal ganglia) at 24 h and 72 h following ICH. d and e, corner turn and forelimb placing were performed at day 0, day 1 and day 3. f, NDS was measured after different operation. n = 6 experiments each group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005 vs Sham group, # p < 0.05, ## p < 0.01, ### p < 0.005 vs ICH group, one-way ANOVA and post hoc Dunnett's test.

Biodistribution of 125I-labeled EGCG or EGCG-CQDs in ICH rats. Time-dependent biodistribution in liver (a), spleen (b) and brain (c). n = 6 experiments each group. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.005, paired samples t-test.
Long-term biocompatibility of EGCG-CQDs. a, concentrations of blood biochemistry parameters in EGCG-CQDs groups. b, H&E staining of the major organs. Scale bar: 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots amplify beneficial effects of EGCG against neural injuries by NLRP3 inflammasome after intracerebral hemorrhage

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Neuroinflammation plays an indispensable role in neural damages after ICH, responsible for the induced high mortality and poor prognosis. NLRP3 inflammasome, which is known mediated by ROS, has been widely documented to aggravate brain injuries. Therefore, suppressing neural injuries by ROS/NLRP3 pathway may be beneficial in treating ICH. As the major catechin found in green tea, epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) shows excellent anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory effects. In this study, EGCG-carbon quantum dots (EGCG-CQDs) were successfully fabricated based on EGCG by hydrothermal synthesis method. EGCG-CQDs exhibited an excellent aqueous solubility, and emerged more abundant phenolic oxygens as well as oxygen-rich functional groups. Importantly, EGCG-CQDs showed superior free radical scavenging activity by DPPH and ABTS assays in vitro than EGCG. In vivo, a significant antioxidative activity was presented by EGCG-CQDs rather than EGCG. Furthermore, the upregulated NLRP3 and the induced inflammatory cascades (NF-κB, Caspase-1 and GSDMD) in ICH were attenuated by EGCG-CQDs. Inflammatory factor productions were also decreased by EGCG-CQDs, such as IL-1β, IL-18, IL-6 and TNF-α. Finally, the disturbed neural viability, disordered cytomorphology, and neurological deficits were significantly improved by EGCG-CQDs rather than EGCG. Therefore, CQDs might be an effective form to amplify the efficacy and bioavailability of EGCG, exerting considerable effects on treating ICH by suppressing ROS/NLRP3.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Stroke, as the leading cause of death, is associated with high disability, mortality and cognitive decline in China (Tu et al., 2023). Though the functional outcomes of ischemic stroke are improved over the past 20 years, there are no decisively effective therapeutic strategies for intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH) (Toyoda et al., 2022). The secondary injuries from the coagulation cascade (especially thrombin), hemoglobin breakdown products and the following inflammation are the leading damage factors in ICH (Sheth, 2022). The therapeutic targets accounting of these potential mechanisms can pave the way for the management of ICH.

Neuroinflammation has been widely documented in the insults after ICH (Tschoe et al., 2020). It is worth noting that NLRP3 inflammasome is the primary element triggering the following inflammatory cascade reactions and worsening neural injuries (Ma et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2020). It contains a sensor (NLRP3), an adapter protein (ACS) and an effector (Caspase-1) (Fusco et al., 2020). Meanwhile, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are demonstrated to be the key inducers of secondary injury and mediate the activation of inflammatory reactions (Qu et al., 2016). Subsequently, the activated NLRP3 inflammasome results in the upregulation of Caspase-1 as well as other inflammatory factors. Over the past several years, growing numbers of researches were focused on the injury mechanisms and the related treatments through NLRP3 inflammasome in ICH (Ma et al., 2014). The brain damages were proved to be alleviated via restraining or interfering ROS/NLRP3 pathway (Xiao et al., 2020).

In our daily diet, tea is one of the most consumed beverages, second only to water (Kochman et al., 2020). Epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) is the main extract from the natural green tea (Chen et al., 2015). EGCG has been confirmed to have potential effects on varieties of diseases in our previous studies and other researches (Chakrawarti et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2015). It provides a considerable resistance to oxidative stress by eliminating ROS and facilitating antioxidant activity, also prevents inflammatory damage by exerting influence on the related cascades. Especially in the central nervous system (CNS) disorders, EGCG is well known due to its beneficial neuroprotective effects (Bao et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2017). However, its limitations are noted, such as the poor bioavailability, inherent instability and even high dose toxicity in vivo (Chakrawarti et al., 2016; Kucera et al., 2015). Especially for the neurological disorders, EGCG shows the restricted efficacy due to its limited penetration (Waris et al., 2022). Besides, researchers realized that the managements failed to obtain favorable outcomes just by the anti-oxidative effects of agents (Yang et al., 2015). So, these challenges are on the top list of considerations in improving EGCG into a promising therapeutic agent. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as fluorescent nano-particles (<10 nm), are gaining substantial attention because of their uniform particle size, low toxicity, high-water solubility and excellent biocompatibility (Das et al., 2018). These particles have been used in various fields widely, such as, bioimaging, medical diagnosis, drug delivery and diseases therapy. They also showed an impressive capability to penetrate the blood–brain barrier (BBB) in the neurological disorders (Bhatia et al., 2024).

Herein, the present study fabricated CQDs based on EGCG to suppress neural injuries through NLRP3 after ICH. EGCG-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis method, and revealed the superior free radical scavenging activity in vitro and vivo. Compared with EGCG, EGCG-CQDs exhibited better antioxidative effects in enhancing GSH and SOD2, reducing LDH and MDA in vivo. Furthermore, our study indicated that EGCG-CQDs decreased the expression of NLRP3 as well as NLRP3 related factors after ICH. Subsequently, EGCG-CQDs alleviated neural damages significantly in vivo. Therefore, the construction of CQDs could potentially enhance the efficacy and bioavailability of EGCG in vivo, and EGCG-CQDs might be a promising candidate for the treatment of ICH.

EGCG-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis method. 10 mg EGCG (Sigma, Germany) was dissolved by 5 mL ddH2O and heated at 150 °C, 180 °C or 210 °C for 12 h. The solution was centrifuged at 10 000 g for 15 min, and then dialyzed by dialysis membrane (MWCO = 0.5–1 kDa) (Aladdin) for 24 h. The purified EGCG-CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C or managed by freeze drying.

The diameter, zeta potential and polydispersity index (PDI) of CQDs were measured by the Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CQDs were recorded by JEM-F200 (Jeol, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was detected by Nicolet iS10 (Thermo scientific, USA). UV–vis absorption was monitored by Cary 5000 (Agilent, USA). All fluorescence spectra were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Primary cortical neuronal cultures and animal preparation
ce:section.ce:para: All procedures were in accordance with the institutional guidelines for animal experimentation and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Children's Hospital of Chongqing Medical University (NO. 2022 0117 007). The primary cortical neuronal cultures were according to the previous method (Guo et al., 2018). Cortex from D18-19 embryos carried by SD dam was removed, cut into pieces and digested. Then cell pellets were resuspended and cultured with neurobasal media (Gibco, USA) with 2 % B27 (Gibco), 0.5 mM GlutaMAX (Gibco) and 0.5 % penicillin–streptomycin (Gibco) after 4 h. Cortical neurons were cultured for 7 days for the last experiments.

ICH model was performed from an established protocol (Guo et al., 2018). Male SD rats weighted 250–300 g. Briefly, needle was inserted into the right striatum in 0.2 mm anterior, 5.5 mm ventral and 4.0 mm lateral to bregma. Rats were injected with 100 µL autologous whole blood. Six rats were used each group. In this study, 127 rats were used and 9 rats were excluded due to death or failure ICH model construction.

In vitro experiments: Primary cortical neurons were randomly divided into seven groups: control group (Con), Hb group (50 µM hemoglobin dissolved in culture medium) (Sigma, USA), EGCG group (0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 and 1000 µM EGCG dissolved in culture medium with Hb), EGCG-CQDs group (0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100 and 1000 µM EGCG-CQDs dissolved in culture medium with Hb), MCC950 group (10 µM MCC950 dissolved in culture medium with Hb) (Sigma) (Li et al., 2022). Cells were incubated for 24 h.

In vivo experiments: Rats were anesthetized by chloral hydrate at 100 mg/kg, and randomly divided into four groups: sham group (needle insertion only), ICH group, EGCG group and EGCG-CQDs group. Sham and ICH groups were intraperitoneally injected with 0.9 % sodium chloride for 3 days. EGCG and EGCG-CQDs groups were intraperitoneally injected with EGCG and EGCG-CQDs at a dose of 0.1, 1, 10, 25, 50 and 100 mg/kg/d for 3 days, respectively. MCC950 group was intraperitoneally injected with MCC950 at a dose of 5 mg/kg/d for 3 days (Fig. 1 ).

EGCG and EGCG-CQDs (0–100 µM) were reacted with 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) (Aladdin) and 2,2′-Azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline −6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt) (ABTS) (Aladdin) in dark at 37 °C for 30 min, and then detected at 517 nm or 730 nm by spectrophotometry. Ascorbic acid was used as the positive control. IC50 value was calculated by Graph Pad 9.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS, LDH, MDA, GSH and neuron viability
ce:section.ce:para: Following the manufacturers’ instructions, ROS, LDH, MDA and GSH was determined by assay kit (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, China). Cell viability was evaluated by CCK-8 (Dojindo, Japan).
After different operations above, brains were divided into 2 hemispheres along the midline after 24 h or 72 h to detect the brain water content according to the previous study (Guo et al., 2018).
Brains were fixed by paraformaldehyde, wrapped by paraffin, and then stained with hematoxylin-eosin (H&E staining).

Neurological deficits were assessed by the corner turn and fore limb placing tests (Luo et al., 2017). Neurologic Deficit Scale (NDS) was measured as the previous method (MacLellan et al., 2006). Test included: spontaneous circling, hind limb retraction, bilateral forepaw grasp, contralateral forelimb flexion and beam walking ability. Score of the impairment was maximum of 14. All tests were randomly assigned.

EGCG and EGCG-CQDs were labeled by 125I according to our and other previous studies (Cheng et al., 2018; Tao et al., 2012). EGCG or EGCG-CQDs, Na125I and chloramine-T (Sigma) were reacted in PBS (0.02 M, pH 7.5) for 30 min. After ICH operation, rats were intraperitoneally administered with 60 μci 125I-labeled EGCG or EGCG-CQDs, then sacrificed at 5 h, 1 d, 3 d and 7 d with major organs collected for gamma counting (Symbia T2, Siemens, USA).

Healthy SD rats were intraperitoneally administrated with PBS or EGCG-CQDs of different concentrations (0.1/1/10/100 mg/kg) every day. At 14 d, the blood was obtained for biochemical analysis. The major organs were collected for H&E staining.

RNA of neurons or perihematomal tissues were isolated by Trizol reagent (TaKaRa, China), and then were reverse-transcribed to cDNA. RT-qPCR was performed with SYBR Green (TaKaRa). The primer sequences showed in Table S1.
The ipsilateral hemisphere tissues were centrifuged at 10 000 g for 10 min, and then ROS, IL-1β, IL-18, IL-6 and TNF-α were measured via ELISA kits (mibio, China).

Cell or tissue sample was incubated with NLRP3 (AdipoGen, USA), NeuN (Abcam, USA) or Caspase-1 p20 (Santa Cruz, USA), then followed by the secondary antibody (Beyotime) or Tunel (Roche, Germany). All samples were counterstained with DAPI (Beyotime). Representative fluorescence images were photographed by confocal microscope (Leica), and quantified by ImageJ (Guo et al., 2024).

Proteins of cell or tissues were extracted, separated by 10 % SDS-PAGE and transferred to PVDF membranes. The first antibodies were Nrf-2 (Abcam), HO-1 (Abcam), SOD2 (Abcam), NLRP3, NF-κB p-p65 (Abcam), NF-κB p65 (Abcam), p-IκBα (Abcam), IκBα (Abcam), Caspase-1 p20, pro-Caspase-1 (Santa Cruz), GSDMD (Abcam) or β-actin (CST, USA) overnight at 4 °C, followed by the secondary antibody (Beyotime). Bands were visualized by ECL (Thermo, USA) and quantified by Quantity-one. Representative strips were proceeded with Adobe Illustration.

All data were presented as means ± SEM. A paired-samples T test was used for two groups, and ANOVA followed Tukey post hoc test for more than two groups were performed using SPSS 20.0 software. Difference with a value of p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All experiments were performed at least 6 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of EGCG-derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: EGCG-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis method at 150 °C, 180 °C or 210 °C respectively for 12 h. CQDs were fabricated through crosslinking, carbonization, and surface passivation (Lim et al., 2015). Results showed that all of CQDs had excellent aqueous solubility and the product yields were in the range of 10–20 % (Table S2). EGCG-CQDs-150, EGCG-CQDs-180 and EGCG-CQDs-210 were uniform spheres with diameters of 9.034 ± 1.628, 4.168 ± 1.238 and 4.043 ± 1.390 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 a, Fig. 2b, Fig. 2c, Fig. 2d and Table S2). EGCG were inclined to carbonized and broken into smaller particles. Zeta potential of these CQDs were + 27.72 ± 2.172, +29.47 ± 2.942 and + 29.67 ± 1.273 with no significant difference (Fig. 2c, Fig. 2e and Table S2). Specifically, the positive particles preferred to be absorbed through cell surface.

Furthermore, FT-IR spectra was conducted to measure the transformed functional groups after different treatment (Fig. 2f). Both EGCG and EGCG-CQDs showed an O–H stretching vibration peak at ≈3600 cm−1 because of the abundant phenolic oxygen which was responsible for the spectacular antioxidant capabilities. C = C and C–H stretching peaks underwent changes but didn’t disappear. In UV–vis absorption, EGCG-CQDs also exhibited a band in the 300–360 nm range, attributed to C = O bonds, indicating the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups (Fig. 2g). Besides, the fluorescence spectra were changed remarkably after carbonization (Fig. 2h). Therefore, EGCG-CQDs could be synthesized from 150 °C to 210 °C.

As the antioxidative compound, EGCG played a vital role in scavenging free radicals (Macedo et al., 2011). In this study, we firstly detected scavenging activity of EGCG and EGCG-CQDs at the concentration of 0–100 µM by DPPH and ABTS assays (Fig. 3 a and 3b). Both of them exhibited excellent inhibiting effect compared with ascorbic acid. EGCG-CQDs-180 decreased DPPH radicals with the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of 9.35 ± 1.36 µM. This group had statistical significance versus EGCG (11.99 ± 1.21 µM), EGCG-CQDs-150 (12.38 ± 1.17 µM) and EGCG-CQDs-210 (13.18 ± 1.9 µM) (p < 0.05), respectively (Fig. 3a). IC50 of scavenging ABTS for EGCG, EGCG-CQDs-150, EGCG-CQDs-180, EGCG-CQDs-210 and ascorbic acid were 0.5649 ± 0.0937, 0.5628 ± 0.1937, 0.3041 ± 0.1219, 0.6893 ± 0.1406 and 0.2384 ± 0.0936 µM, respectively (Fig. 3b). In the process of transformation from EGCG to EGCG-CQDs, a higher degree of condensation was induced by the higher temperature. On the other hand, C = C stretching, C–O stretching and C–H bending indicated that the pyrolyzed products of EGCG still remained on the CQDs surfaces (Lin et al., 2019). As revealed in the above results, EGCG-CQDs-180 represented better anti-oxidative effect compared to EGCG-CQDs-150 and EGCG-CQDs-210. So, we chosen EGCG-CQDs-180 for the following experiments and defined this group as EGCG-CQDs.

To further elucidate the influence of EGCG and EGCG-CQDs on the scavenging activity, we measured ROS content of primary cortical neurons induced by Hb (50 µM) in vitro (Fig. 3c, Fig. 3d and Fig. S1). In this study, Hb elevated ROS content significantly. EGCG-CQDs, as well as EGCG, showed extremely remarkable difference compared with Hb group especially at the concentration of 1, 10 and 100 µM (Fig. 3d). However, EGCG-CQDs could reduce ROS content remarkably in rats following ICH, was superior to the unprocessed EGCG at the dose of 25 and 50 mg/kg/d (Fig. 3e and Fig. S2). These observations forcefully supported that EGCG-CQDs could improve the capacity of EGCG to scavenge free radicals in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EGCG-CQDs suppressed oxidative stress related factors
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the related factors induced by excessive ROS were detected (Fig. 4, Fig. S3 and S4). After the insult, neurons or neural tissues released much more LDH (Fig. 4 a, Fig. 4d and Fig. S3a). Additionally, MDA, the lipid oxidative end product, was upregulated significantly (Fig. 4b, Fig. 4e and Fig. S3a). GSH was disturbed when disposed to hemoglobin (Fig. 4c, Fig. 4f and Fig. S3b). Both EGCG and EGCG-CQDs attenuated the induced oxidative stress at 10 and 100 µM compared with Hb group in vitro (Fig. 4a, Fig. 4b and Fig. 4c). In vivo, compared to ICH group, the expressions of genes related to oxidative stress were significantly distinct in EGCG and EGCG-CQDs both at 25 and 50 mg/kg/d (Fig. S3a and Figure S3b). EGCG-CQDs performed superior effects on the release of LDH, MDA and GSH compared to EGCG at 25 mg/kg/d (Fig. 4d, Fig. 4e and Fig. 4f). Taking the above results together, we chose EGCG and EGCG-CQDs of 10 µM in vitro and 25 mg/kg/d in vivo for the following experiments.

Besides, as the key element in the cellular defense mechanism to maintain redox homeostasis, Nrf-2/HO-1 signal pathway was also investigated in the present study (Kim et al., 2022). Results indicated that this mentioned pathway and SOD2 were remarkably inhibited in Hb group (Fig. 4g, Fig. 4h and Fig. S4) and ICH group (Fig. 4i, Fig. 4j and Fig. S3b), respectively. Similarly, these antioxidants were increased dramatically by both EGCG and EGCG-CQDs in vitro, however EGCG failed to exert the comparable effects in vivo. Accordingly, enhancing EGCG delivery to brain tissues by CQDs might improve EGCG’s bioavailability.

Despite the superior resistance to NLRP3 inflammasome of EGCG and EGCG-CQDs in vitro, only EGCG-CQDs had the considerable effects in vivo. All the available results revealed that the anti-inflammatory effect of EGCG was reinforced by CQDs-based strategy.

To further elucidate the effect of EGCG and EGCG-CQDs on neural injuries, we investigated cell viability of neurons and neurologic deficits of the experimental rats. In primary cortical cells, EGCG and its quantum dots form could increase the downregulated cell viability significantly (Fig. 7 a and Fig. S1b). Essential to the integrity of cellular morphology and tissue architecture, NeuN, MAP-2, NCAM-L1 and GAP-43 were impaired by Hb significantly, yet reinforced by EGCG and EGCG-CQDs notably (Fig. 7b). What’s more, the number of processes and dendrite length were increased to maintain the connection and adhesion of neurons in these two groups (Fig. 7c, 7d and 7e).

In rat ICH model, the perihematomal neural cells were morphologically normal in Sham using H&E staining. However, nuclear pyknosis, karyorrhexis and karyolysis were observed in ICH group (Fig. 8 a). EGCG-CQDs was more favorable to improve these nuclear morphological abnormalities compared with EGCG. Meanwhile, EGCG-CQDs had a remarkable effect on the perihematomal edema at 24 h and 72 h following ICH (Fig. 8b and 8c). Regarding the neurological function, a higher conner turn as well as NDS score, and lower forelimb placing were exhibited in ICH group (Fig. 8d, Fig. 8e, Fig. 8f and Fig. S2b). No statistically significant difference was found between ICH and EGCG group. However, neurological function impairments were improved dramatically in EGCG-CQDs group. As a result, CQDs enhanced the beneficial effects of EGCG to improve brain injuries after ICH.

We detected the biodistribution of 125I-labeled EGCG or EGCG-CQDs. Results indicated that they mainly accumulated in the RES organs, such as liver and spleen (Fig. 9 a and 9b). The reduced radio activities were observed in these organs. In brain tissues, the percentage of labeled EGCG-CQDs were much higher than EGCG at 5 h, 1 d, 3 d and 7 d following ICH (Fig. 9c). Therefore, EGCG might be facilitated to target to brain tissues by CQDs strategy.

Additionally, the safety of EGCG-CQDs was also conducted. After long-term administration, serum biochemical factors had no significant difference in these groups, such as, alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), triglyceride (TG) and albumin (ALB) (Fig. 10 a). In H&E staining, no structure and pathological changes were observed (Fig. 10b).

Neuroinflammation is accountable for the neural damages after ICH leading to high mortality and poor prognosis (Sheth, 2022). However, no available and effective management specifically focused on neuroinflammation. Suppressing NLRP3 inflammasome and the related inflammatory cytokines has been widely documented to improve neurological injuries as a promising treatment strategy for ICH (Guo et al., 2024; Xiao et al., 2020). Since ROS is the critical mediators of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, suppressing ROS/NLRP3 pathway may be beneficial to alleviate inflammation-induced damages following ICH (Dominic et al., 2022).. In this study, EGCG-CQDs, the fabricated CQDs based on EGCG, showed an extraordinary free radical scavenging activity in vitro and vivo. Furthermore, it attenuated NLRP3 and the following inflammatory cascades significantly. Additionally, the disturbed neural viability, disordered cytomorphology and neurological deficits in ICH were improved by EGCG-CQDs dramatically. Therefore, CQDs might be an effective strategy to increase the efficacy and bioavailability of EGCG by suppressing ROS/NLRP3 to refine prognosis for ICH.

Clinically, overdosed ROS is observed in patients following ICH and is associated with the secondary brain injury, therefore ROS scavengers can minimize the subsequent injury to optimize outcomes for patients with ICH (Kim et al., 2021). Besides, as an independent role in the innate immune system, NLRP3 inflammasome is reported to be promoted by ROS (Gong et al., 2018). The stimulated ROS reduces the membrane potential and destructs cellular balance to boost the inflammatory responses. Therefore, the specific anti-oxidants are crucial in treating ICH (Alfonso-Loeches et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2020). In this study, EGCG-CQDs showed a superior anti-oxidative effect of scavenging free radicals in vitro and vivo than EGCG (Fig. 3). Besides, oxidative products were also diminished by EGCG-CQDs, such as, LDH and MDA (Fig. 4). Nrf-2 is the regulator of cellular oxidative stress and initiates the following anti-oxidative pathways, especially HO-1 (Kim et al., 2022). HO-1 catalyzes the conversion of heme, and mitigates the toxicity associated with heme which causes oxidative stress and neural impairments in ICH (Yao et al., 2021). Therefore, with antioxidative effects by reducing ROS, Nrf-2/HO-1 pathway possesses a major influence on promoting neuroprotection and improving outcomes of ICH. The anti-oxidative factors and Nrf-2/HO-1 pathway were also facilitated by EGCG-CQDs to resist neurological injuries (Fig. 4).

NLRP3 inflammasome is known as an essential contributor to the ICH-induced inflammatory reactions (Ma et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2020). Notably, the subsequent NLRP3 and cell damage can be activated by excessive ROS directly, promoting inflammatory responses. More intervening measures against NLRP3-related brain damage are demanded. MCC950, a specific small-molecule inhibitor that selectively blocks NLRP3, is reported to reduce neuroinflammation and improve long-term neurological outcomes in brain diseases (Li et al., 2022). In this study, EGCG-CQDs, like MCC950, suppressed the increased NLRP3-positive neural cells and NLRP3 expression (Fig. 5). The activated NLRP3 leads to the release of inflammatory cytokines via NF-κB. Caspase-1 is also an active participant in the procedure. Here, the upregulated inflammasome related factors were also diminished by CQDs and MCC950 (Fig. 6). EGCG-CQDs decreased Caspase-1- and Tunel-positive cells, as well as the expression of NF-κB p-p65, p-IκBα and GSDMD. Besides, the cytokines, IL-1β, IL-18, IL-6 and TNF-α, were also reduced significantly. Therefore, similar to MCC950, EGCG-CQDs could attenuate NLRP3 and its induced inflammatory cascades after ICH. As a consequence, neural cells viability was boosted, neural structure was maintained and neurological deficits were improved by EGCG-CQDs (Figs. 7 and 8). Therefore, EGCG-CQDs suppressed NLRP3 and its related inflammatory factors by antioxidative effects following ICH.

However, our available results pointed to the fact that even though EGCG had anti-oxidative effects in vitro, these effects were shrunk in the ICH rat model and EGCG failed to improve neurological injuries in vivo. As a contrast, EGCG-CQDs could break this limit to lower oxidative stress, reduce NLRP3 inflammasome and the related inflammatory factors, and facilitate better outcomes following ICH in vivo. The efficacy and bioavailability of EGCG was reinforced by CQDs strategy (Figs. 8 and 9). But how CQDs strategy exerted these amplified effects? Firstly, oxygen-rich functional groups in UV–vis absorption and abundant phenolic oxygens in FT-IR spectra were present in EGCG-CQDs, which were responsible for the spectacular antioxidant capabilities in vitro and vivo (Fig. 2). Additionally, the zeta potential of EGCG-CQDs-180 was + 29.47 ± 2.942 mV which displayed the positive surface charges. These particles were inclined to bind to and taken in by neural cells through electrostatic association, a kind of physical adhesion, which was the primary factor in the process of translocation (Zhang et al., 2018). Moreover, with the relatively small size (4.168 ± 1.238 nm), CQDs achieved better cell affinity when positively rather than negatively charged. Despite the short circulation time of small molecular drugs, the pharmacokinetics will be enhanced by nanosized strategy which termed “passive” targeting. In some specific microenvironment, this kind of targeting can be facilitated (Attia et al., 2019). In ICH, ROS might be the enhancer to increase the enhanced permeation and retention of EGCG-CQDs. Lastly, CQDs are low toxic, water soluble, physiochemically and photochemically stable, with an excellent biocompatibility (Lim et al., 2015). As a result, the nanoparticles strategy increased the bioavailability of EGCG after ICH (Figs. 8, 9 and 10).

In this study, we fabricated CQDs based on EGCG by hydrothermal synthesis method. EGCG-CQDs exhibited a superior free radical scavenging activity in vitro and vivo. Through the anti-oxidative effects, EGCG-CQDs decreased NLRP3 inflammasome and the related factors after ICH. Further study showed that EGCG-CQDs maintained neural architecture and improved neurological functions. Therefore, compared to EGCG, EGCG-CQDs could enhance the efficacy and bioavailability by CQDs-based strategy, and EGCG-CQDs might be a promising candidate for ICH treatment.

ce:para.ce:bold: Ethics approval and consent to participate

ce:section.ce:para: All procedures were in accordance with the institutional guidelines for animal experimentation and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Children’s Hospital of Chongqing Medical University (NO. 2022 0117 007). This study was reported in accordance with ARRIVE guidelines.

Min Xiao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yun Pan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shijin Tang: . Tingwang Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lin Yang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Gang Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11902060), the Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology (Grant Nos. cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0844 and cstc2020jcyjmsxmX0250), the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (Grant No. KJQN201900814, KJQN202100837 and KJZD-K202400805) and the Opening Research Platform of Chongqing Technology and Business University (KFJJ2022016).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2025.125281.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Fig. 1
EGCG-CQDs decreased the intracellular ROS content and increased cell viability in vitro.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EGCG-CQDs decreased ROS content in the perihematomal brain tissues and increased NDS score in vivo.
The mR NA expression in the perihematomal tissues after ICH in vivo.
EGCG-CQDs suppressed other inflammatory pathways in vitro and vivo.
